primary_diagnosis.name: Acute deep vein thrombosis of the right lower limb (provoked by recent long-haul travel)

pathophysiology_summary: Deep vein thrombosis is the formation of a thrombus within the deep venous system, most commonly of the calf/lower limb. Virchow's triad - venous stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability - underlies it; here the dominant provoking factor is venous stasis from prolonged immobility during a 14-hour long-haul flight three days before onset. Obstruction of venous outflow produces the characteristic unilateral findings: calf swelling with a measurable circumference discrepancy (4.5 cm here, well above the >3 cm Wells threshold), a deep aching pain/tenderness along the course of the deep veins (a palpable tender venous cord over the posterior tibial vein), warmth and erythema, distension of superficial collateral veins, and pitting oedema confined to the symptomatic leg. A low-grade temperature can accompany DVT. The critical danger is propagation and embolisation: a fragment of thrombus can dislodge and travel to the pulmonary arteries, causing a pulmonary embolism that may be fatal - which is why massage of the calf is contraindicated and why sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, or haemoptysis are emergency red flags. Diagnosis is stratified with the Wells score (high here) and confirmed by compression duplex ultrasonography demonstrating a non-compressible vein; management is anticoagulation (a direct oral anticoagulant such as rivaroxaban) once the diagnosis is established and bleeding risk assessed. Homan's sign (pain on passive dorsiflexion) is historically described but is neither sensitive nor specific and is not relied upon. Calf-circumference measurement, palpation of a venous cord, and compression ultrasound cannot be performed over telehealth, and anticoagulation should not be initiated remotely without confirming the diagnosis and bleeding risk.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute unilateral painful, swollen, warm, red calf is the classic DVT pattern from the first turn.

Warmth, redness and swelling could indicate cellulitis, but the absence of a skin break, distinct spreading margin or high fever, with a palpable venous cord and large circumference discrepancy, favours DVT.
Can mimic calf DVT, but lack of prior knee joint pathology and the strong immobility risk factor make DVT more likely.
Muscle injury causes calf pain, but the gradual non-exertional onset with venous distension and low-grade fever points away from a simple tear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor should treat this as probable DVT, elicit the immobility risk factor, and recognise the PE risk, while keeping cellulitis and other mimics in mind.

differential.diagnosis: Provoked deep vein thrombosis (right lower limb), high Wells probability
differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral calf swelling (DI-001) + deep calf tenderness (DI-002) + warmth/erythema (DI-003) + recent 14-hour flight (DI-004) with a large calf-circumference discrepancy gives a high Wells score, making DVT highly probable.
Remains a differential to exclude on assessment, but the venous cord, immobility history and circumference discrepancy favour DVT.
No prior knee joint effusion/arthritis history and a strong thrombotic risk factor make this unlikely.
No sudden exertional snap; the venous distension and low-grade fever are not features of a simple muscle tear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen DVT is highly probable and the AI Doctor should escalate for urgent compression ultrasound and anticoagulation while giving explicit PE safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Provoked lower-limb DVT - presumptive, high Wells probability, pending compression duplex ultrasound
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation requires compression duplex ultrasonography (non-compressible vein); anticoagulation is initiated once diagnosis and bleeding risk are assessed in the DVT pathway.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is high-probability DVT requiring urgent same-day assessment (DVT pathway/ED) with ultrasound and anticoagulation, plus explicit PE safety-netting - not a flight cramp to rest at home.

red_flags.description: Acute unilateral calf swelling with marked circumference discrepancy (4.5 cm)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unilateral swelling with a >3 cm discrepancy is a key Wells criterion strongly supporting DVT.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: DVT dismissed as a cramp; thrombus propagates or embolises to the lungs.

red_flags.description: Deep calf tenderness with a palpable tender venous cord along the deep posterior tibial vein
red_flags.clinical_significance: Localised deep venous tenderness/cord supports thrombosis of the deep venous system.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The venous origin of the pain is missed and the clot goes untreated.

red_flags.description: Recent prolonged immobility (14-hour long-haul flight) - major provoking risk factor
red_flags.clinical_significance: Recent immobility is a Wells criterion and the key provoking factor raising the pre-test probability of DVT.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to elicit the risk factor lowers suspicion and delays diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Pulmonary embolism warning symptoms (sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis) - currently absent but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: PE is the life-threatening complication of DVT; explicit safety-netting for its onset is mandatory even when the chest is currently clear.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If PE develops and the patient is not primed to seek emergency help, a fatal embolism may go unrecognised.

red_flags.description: Non-compressible deep vein on compression duplex ultrasonography
red_flags.clinical_significance: A non-compressible vein on ultrasound is the confirmatory diagnostic finding for DVT; it cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - ultrasound requires an in-person service; the correct action is urgent imaging.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attributing the calf pain to a flight cramp or muscle strain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The immobility that causes DVT can be misread as the cause of a benign cramp; the AI Doctor may under-triage a limb-and-life-threatening clot.
With the chest currently clear, the AI Doctor may omit explicit warning about sudden breathlessness/pleuritic chest pain/haemoptysis, missing the chance to catch a PE early.
Rubbing or massaging the calf can dislodge thrombus and precipitate a PE; this must be explicitly discouraged.
Homan's sign is neither sensitive nor specific; using it to rule DVT in or out is unreliable and can mislead.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High probability (>=2 points; features here include unilateral calf swelling with >3 cm discrepancy, localised deep venous tenderness, pitting oedema confined to the symptomatic leg, and recent immobility)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A high Wells probability warrants urgent compression duplex ultrasound and consideration of empirical anticoagulation via the DVT pathway if imaging is delayed and bleeding risk is acceptable.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of a tender venous cord along the deep veins
Formal clinician assessment of erythema margins to distinguish cellulitis
Bleeding-risk assessment and baseline bloods (FBC, renal function, coagulation) before anticoagulation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day assessment via a DVT pathway or emergency department for compression ultrasound and anticoagulation, advising leg elevation and strictly no massage - with an ambulance (000) if sudden breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, or haemoptysis develop (possible PE).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient this is a flight cramp or muscle strain and advising rest/massage at home risks thrombus propagation and a fatal pulmonary embolism.
